26:0.rendering: Yahweh spoke to Moses: "Speak to the Israelites and say to them, 'When someone makes a special vow to dedicate a person to Yahweh, the valuation shall be as follows.'"
26:0.note: This chapter's opening frames vows as a way to dedicate people or things to God, echoing the dedication of the firstborn in Exodus 13.

26:1.rendering: Speak to the Israelites and say to them: When a person makes a special vow to dedicate a human being to the Lord, the valuation shall be based on the standard for persons.
26:1.note: The Hebrew word for "valuation" (erek) echoes the creation language of Genesis, where humans are valued as image-bearers now being deliberately given back to their Maker.

26:2.rendering: Your valuation for a male from twenty years old up to sixty years old shall be fifty shekels of silver, according to the shekel of the sanctuary.
26:2.note: This precise valuation for adult males reflects ancient Near Eastern practices of assessing worth for vows or dedications, often tied to temple economics.

26:3.rendering: If it is a female, then your valuation shall be thirty shekels of silver.
26:3.note: The lower valuation for females reflects ancient economic realities where a woman's labor was assessed differently in sanctuary vows.

26:4.rendering: If the person is between five and twenty years old, the valuation shall be twenty shekels for a male and ten shekels for a female.
26:4.note: These age-based valuations echo the census counts in Numbers, treating people like dedicated war spoils offered back to God.

26:5.rendering: For a person between one month and five years old, the valuation shall be five silver shekels for a male and three silver shekels for a female.
26:5.note: This sliding scale of worth by age and gender reflects ancient economic valuations for redemption vows, not modern ideas of human dignity.

26:6.rendering: For a person sixty years old or more, the valuation for a male shall be fifteen shekels, and for a female ten shekels.
26:6.note: This sliding scale for the elderly reflects ancient Israel's realistic view of diminished strength in old age while still affirming their worth in the sanctuary's economy.

26:7.rendering: But if he is too poor to pay your valuation, then he shall be presented before the priest, and the priest shall set a value for him according to what the one who made the vow can afford.
26:7.note: In the ancient world of fixed vows, this law carves out economic mercy, letting the priest calibrate the price to the person's actual capacity rather than a rigid formula.

25:0.rendering: You shall not make idols for yourselves, nor set up a carved image or sacred pillar for yourselves, nor install a figured stone in your land to bow down to it. For I am the LORD your God.
25:0.note: The Hebrew word for "idols" here echoes the golden calf incident, warning Israel against repeating that ancient Near Eastern temptation right in their own promised land.

25:1.rendering: You shall keep my Sabbaths and reverence my sanctuary. I am the Lord.
25:1.note: The Sabbath command here echoes Exodus 20:8 but adds the sanctuary, tying weekly rest to the sacred space where God's presence dwells.

25:2.rendering: If you walk in my statutes and keep my commands and do them,
25:2.note: The Hebrew for "walk" echoes the very first command in Eden, inviting Israel to retrace humanity's original path of trust.

25:3.rendering: I will give you rain in its season, so the land will yield its harvest and the trees of the field will give their fruit.
25:3.note: The phrase "in its season" echoes the orderly rhythms of creation in Genesis 1, where God sets times for fruitfulness after the exodus people keep covenant.

25:4.rendering: Your threshing will reach into the vintage, and the vintage will reach into the sowing. You will eat your bread to the full, and you will dwell securely in your land.
25:4.note: This verse weaves a single flowing harvest where one season bleeds into the next, painting a picture of overflowing abundance that echoes the original blessing of Eden.

25:5.rendering: I will grant peace in the land, and you will lie down with nothing to frighten you. I will remove the dangerous animals from the land, and no sword will pass through your country.
25:5.note: The Hebrew word for "peace" here is shalom, which echoes the original harmony of Eden and points forward to the rest promised in the land.

25:6.rendering: You will chase down your enemies, and they will fall before you by the sword.
25:6.note: This promise of pursuit and victory echoes the ancient Near Eastern covenant pattern where obedience brings military dominance, a thread that runs from Deuteronomy back to the patriarchal blessings.

25:7.rendering: Five of you will chase a hundred, and a hundred of you will chase ten thousand, and your enemies will fall before you by the sword.
25:7.note: This hyperbolic promise of multiplied strength echoes the ancient covenant formula where obedience turns the few into a devastating force against the many.

26:8.rendering: If it is an animal that one may present as an offering to the LORD, anything of it that one gives to the LORD shall be holy.
26:8.note: The Hebrew word for "holy" here (qodesh) means "set apart," turning an ordinary animal into untouchable property once vowed.

26:9.rendering: He must not replace it or exchange it, whether good for bad or bad for good. If he does exchange one animal for another, then both it and the one exchanged for it shall be holy.
26:9.note: The word for "exchange" echoes the same root used for redeeming firstborn sons or animals elsewhere in Leviticus, showing how this vow system protects against human schemes to game the sanctuary.

26:10.rendering: But if it is any kind of unclean animal that may not be presented as an offering to the LORD, then the owner shall set the animal before the priest.
26:10.note: The Hebrew repeats "unclean" three times in verses 11-13, underscoring how even an unacceptable animal can still be redeemed through priestly valuation.

26:11.rendering: The priest shall assess it, whether it is good or bad, and according to the priest's assessment, so it shall be.
26:11.note: In the original Hebrew, the priest's valuation hinges on the same root word for "assess" that echoes the book's opening command to "count" the people, turning personal vows into a measured act of worship.

26:12.rendering: If he really wants to redeem it, he must add a fifth of its value to the assessment.
26:12.note: The extra fifth acts like a penalty fee, turning redemption into a costly act of reclaiming what was set apart.

26:13.rendering: When someone dedicates their house as holy to the LORD, the priest shall assess it, whether good or bad. As the priest assesses it, so it shall stand.
26:13.note: In the ancient world, dedicating a house meant transferring its value irrevocably to the sanctuary, like handing over the deed itself.

26:14.rendering: If the one who dedicates his house wishes to redeem it, he must add a fifth of the valuation money to it, and it will belong to him again.
26:14.note: The extra fifth, a recurring echo throughout Leviticus 27, trains Israel in the cost of reclaiming what was set apart for God.

26:15.rendering: If someone dedicates part of the field they own to the LORD, its value shall be calculated according to the amount of seed required for it: fifty shekels of silver for a homer of barley seed.
26:15.note: The valuation ties the field's worth directly to its agricultural yield potential, reflecting an ancient economy where land was measured by what it could produce.

26:16.rendering: If he dedicates his field from the year of jubilee, the valuation shall stand according to your assessment.
26:16.note: The valuation holds firm when dedicated right at the jubilee, the moment when all land returns to its original owners.

26:17.rendering: But if he dedicates his field after the Jubilee, then the priest shall calculate the money according to the years that remain until the next Jubilee year, and a deduction shall be made from your valuation.
26:17.note: The priest subtracts value year by year until the next Jubilee, showing how the sacred calendar shapes every financial commitment.

26:18.rendering: If the one who dedicated the field redeems it, he must add a fifth of the silver of the valuation to it, and it will belong to him.
26:18.note: The added fifth acts like a built-in penalty for reclaiming what you once gave to God.

26:19.rendering: But if he does not redeem the field, or if he has sold the field to another man, it shall not be redeemed anymore.
26:19.note: The verse turns on the Hebrew word ga'al (redeem), echoing the kinsman-redeemer theme that runs from Leviticus to Ruth and Isaiah.

26:20.rendering: But the field, when it is released in the Jubilee, shall be holy to the LORD, like a field that has been devoted. The priest shall take possession of it as his property.
26:20.note: In the Jubilee year this devoted field becomes permanently holy, a striking echo of how the land itself can mirror the total dedication of a person or animal in the chapter's opening verses.

26:21.rendering: If he dedicates to the LORD a field that he has bought, which is not part of his inherited land,
26:21.note: This verse shifts from family inheritance to purchased land, highlighting how even acquired property could be set apart for God in ancient Israel.

26:22.rendering: The priest shall calculate for him the value of the field based on the years remaining until the Jubilee year, and he shall give that valuation on that day as a holy offering to the Lord.
26:22.note: This verse shows the priest acting as a living calculator, adjusting the field's value precisely by the remaining years to Jubilee, echoing the book's opening command to count time in sacred cycles.

26:23.rendering: In the year of jubilee the field shall return to the one from whom it was bought, to the one who owns the land as a hereditary possession.
26:23.note: The jubilee law returns sold land to its original family line, echoing God's vision of Israel as tenants on his inheritance rather than permanent owners.

25:8.rendering: I will turn toward you, make you fruitful and multiply you, and establish my covenant with you.
25:8.note: The verbs echo God's original blessing to humanity in Genesis 1:28, now renewed as covenant promise to a people who have just received the law.

25:9.rendering: You will eat the old harvest that has been stored away, and you will clear out the old to make room for the new.
25:9.note: This promise of overflowing abundance echoes the manna story, where God made the old spoil to welcome the fresh provision each day.

25:10.rendering: I will set my dwelling place among you, and my soul will not abhor you.
25:10.note: The Hebrew word for "dwelling place" is mishkan, the very term for God's tabernacle, hinting that the whole land will become a sanctuary.

25:11.rendering: I will walk about among you, and I will be your God, and you will be my people.
25:11.note: This echoes God's original desire to dwell with humanity in Eden, now promised to an entire covenant people in the wilderness.

25:12.rendering: I am the LORD your God, who brought you out from the land of Egypt so you would no longer be their slaves. I broke the bars of your yoke and made you walk upright.
25:12.note: The word for "yoke" echoes the crushing labor of Egypt in Exodus, turning a symbol of oppression into a promise of restored dignity.

25:13.rendering: But if you will not listen to me and will not do all these commands,
25:13.note: This verse pivots the entire chapter from blessing to curse, echoing the covenant pattern of Deuteronomy 28.

25:14.rendering: and if you reject my statutes, and your soul abhors my rules, so that you will not do all my commandments, but break my covenant,
25:14.note: The Hebrew piles up five escalating actions, from passive neglect to active loathing and covenant rupture.

25:15.rendering: I will appoint sudden terror over you, wasting disease and fever that consume the eyes and cause the throat to pine away. You will sow your seed in vain, for your enemies will eat it.
25:15.note: The Hebrew word for "sudden terror" (behala) echoes the panic of a defeated army, turning covenant curses into a portrait of national invasion and futile labor.

26:24.rendering: Every valuation of yours shall be according to the sanctuary shekel. The shekel shall be twenty gerahs.
26:24.note: This standard for the sanctuary shekel echoes Exodus 30:13, anchoring every vowed valuation to the same measure used for the tabernacle tax and priestly service.

26:25.rendering: However, the firstborn of an animal, which as firstborn belongs to the LORD, no one may dedicate. Whether ox or sheep, it already belongs to the LORD.
26:25.note: The firstborn already belongs to God by right of creation, so it cannot be vowed as an optional gift.

26:26.rendering: If it is from an unclean animal, then he shall ransom it at your valuation and add one fifth to it. If he does not ransom it, then it shall be sold at your valuation.
26:26.note: Unclean animals could not be offered on the altar, so they were redeemed for their value plus twenty percent or sold.

26:27.rendering: But no devoted thing that a man devotes to the LORD from all that belongs to him, whether from people, animals, or the field of his inheritance, shall be sold or redeemed. Every devoted thing is most holy to the LORD.
26:27.note: The Hebrew word for "devoted" (cherem) echoes the total destruction commanded in holy war, now repurposed for irreversible gifts that cannot be bought back.

26:28.rendering: No devoted thing that a man devotes to the Lord from among all that belongs to him, whether man or animal or inherited field, shall be sold or redeemed. Every devoted thing is most holy to the Lord.
26:28.note: The Hebrew herem here marks something irrevocably handed over, often by destruction, echoing the total conquest language of Joshua.

26:29.rendering: Every tenth of the land's seed, from the land's produce, or from the fruit of the tree, belongs to the LORD. It is holy to the LORD.
26:29.note: The word for "tenth" here is ma'aser, the same root used for the king of Sodom's offer in Genesis 14 and Abraham's gift to Melchizedek, quietly threading tithing into Israel's story from the beginning.

26:30.rendering: If a man redeems any of his tithe, he must add a fifth of its value to it.
26:30.note: The added fifth creates a built-in cost to buying back what already belongs to God, echoing the redemption price woven through Leviticus.

26:31.rendering: And every tenth animal from the herd and flock, whatever passes under the shepherd's rod, the tenth one shall be set apart as holy to the LORD.
26:31.note: The shepherd's rod creates a vivid image of counting livestock one by one, echoing the ancient practice of tithing that sets apart the ordinary for God's use.

25:16.rendering: I will set my face against you, and you will be struck down before your enemies. Those who hate you will rule over you, and you will flee even when no one is pursuing you.
25:16.note: The phrase "set my face against you" echoes the high priestly blessing in Numbers 6:25, but here God turns the shining face of favor into its terrifying opposite.

25:17.rendering: And if in spite of these things you will not listen to me, then I will discipline you seven times more for your sins.
25:17.note: The Hebrew "seven times more" carries the idea of complete or full measure, echoing the sevenfold judgments that escalate through this chapter.

25:18.rendering: I will break your stubborn pride, and I will make your sky like iron and your ground like bronze.
25:18.note: The Hebrew word for "pride" here is the same one used for Pharaoh's hard heart in Exodus, linking Israel's rebellion to Egypt's defiance.

25:19.rendering: Your strength will be spent in vain, for your land will not yield its harvest, and the trees of the land will not give their fruit.
25:19.note: The Hebrew verb for "spent in vain" echoes the same word used for futile labor under the curse in Genesis 3:17-19.

25:20.rendering: Yet if you walk against me in opposition and refuse to listen to me, I will add seven times more blows against you according to your sins.
25:20.note: The Hebrew verb for "walk against me" echoes the covenant's call to "walk in my statutes," flipping obedience into defiant opposition.

25:21.rendering: I will send the wild animals against you, and they will rob you of your children, destroy your livestock, and make you few in number so that your roads lie empty.
25:21.note: The Hebrew verb for "make few" echoes the creation mandate to "multiply," turning the blessing of fruitfulness into a curse of depopulation.

25:22.rendering: But if after these things you still act toward me with hostility, and refuse to listen to me, then I will go on striking you, seven times for your sins.
25:22.note: The Hebrew word for "hostility" here echoes the same term used for God's own judgment, creating a mirror effect between Israel's defiance and divine response.

25:23.rendering: Then I myself will walk against you in hostility, and I myself will strike you seven times over for your sins.
25:23.note: The repeated "I myself" and the number seven echo the covenant curses of Deuteronomy 28, turning the Creator into covenant enforcer.

25:24.rendering: I will bring a sword against you that will execute the vengeance of the covenant. When you gather into your cities, I will send plague among you, and you will be given into the hand of the enemy.
25:24.note: The phrase "vengeance of the covenant" links this warning back to the covenant curses in Deuteronomy 28, showing judgment as the dark side of God's unbreakable promise.

25:25.rendering: When I break your staff of bread, ten women will bake your bread in one oven and they will ration it out by weight. You will eat and not be satisfied.
25:25.note: The "staff of bread" echoes the curse in Leviticus 26:26, a picture of famine so severe that even shared ovens cannot satisfy hunger, turning daily provision into measured scarcity.

25:26.rendering: And if in spite of this you will not listen to me, but walk with me in hostility,
25:26.note: The Hebrew word for "hostility" here echoes the same term used for the flaming sword guarding Eden, picturing a fractured relationship with God.

25:27.rendering: I will walk against you in fury, and I myself will discipline you seven times over for your sins.
25:27.note: The Hebrew word for "walk" (halakh) echoes God's earlier promise to "walk among you" in blessing, now flipped to hostile confrontation.

25:28.rendering: You will eat the flesh of your sons, and you will eat the flesh of your daughters.
25:28.note: This horrifying curse echoes the covenant warnings of Deuteronomy 28:53 and finds its echo in the siege accounts of 2 Kings 6 and Lamentations 4, showing how deeply Israel could unravel when the covenant was shattered.

25:29.rendering: I will destroy your high places, cut down your incense altars, and pile your corpses on the lifeless bodies of your idols. My soul will abhor you.
25:29.note: The Hebrew word for "abhor" here echoes God's warning in Leviticus 11 about unclean things, turning covenant rebellion into a kind of ritual defilement that even the land will vomit out.

25:30.rendering: I will turn your sanctuaries into ruins, and I will not smell the pleasing aroma of your offerings.
25:30.note: The phrase "I will not smell" plays on ancient Near Eastern worship language, where gods were said to delight in the scent of sacrifices.

25:31.rendering: I will make your land so desolate that even your enemies who settle in it will be appalled by it.
25:31.note: The Hebrew word for "appalled" here echoes the horror of the land vomiting out its inhabitants, turning even invaders into stunned witnesses of divine judgment.

26:32.rendering: He must not inspect whether it is good or bad, nor swap it. If he does swap it at all, then both it and the one swapped for it will be set apart. It cannot be bought back.
26:32.note: The Hebrew word for "inspect" (baqar) echoes the verb for examining a sacrificial animal for defects, turning this into a wisdom warning against second-guessing what you've already devoted to God.

26:33.rendering: These are the commands that the LORD commanded Moses for the people of Israel on Mount Sinai.
26:33.note: This closing verse frames the entire book of Leviticus as direct speech from God through Moses at Sinai, echoing the covenant scene of Exodus 19-20.

25:32.rendering: I will scatter you among the nations, and I will unsheathe the sword after you. Your land will become a desolation, and your cities will become ruins.
25:32.note: The Hebrew verb for "scatter" echoes the very word used for the dispersion of the nations at Babel, turning covenant breach into a reversal of Israel's exodus gathering.

25:33.rendering: Then the land will enjoy its sabbaths all the days it lies desolate while you are in the land of your enemies. Thus the land will rest and enjoy its sabbaths.
25:33.note: The land itself gets to catch its breath and keep the sabbaths Israel refused it.

25:34.rendering: All the days it lies desolate it shall rest, the rest that it did not rest on your sabbaths when you were dwelling on it.
25:34.note: The land itself gets the sabbath rest Israel refused to give it, echoing the creation rhythm from Genesis 2:2-3.

25:35.rendering: As for those of you who are left, I will send faintness into their hearts in the lands of their enemies. The sound of a driven leaf will chase them, and they will flee as though fleeing from a sword, and they will fall when no one pursues.
25:35.note: The Hebrew word for "faintness" here echoes the terror that fell on Egypt during the plagues, now turned back on a disobedient Israel.

25:36.rendering: They will stumble over one another as if fleeing from the sword, though no one pursues them. You will have no power to stand before your enemies.
25:36.note: The Hebrew word for "stumble" (kashal) echoes the covenant curses in Deuteronomy 28, painting a picture of panic so total that even flight collapses under its own weight.

25:37.rendering: You will perish among the nations, and the land of your enemies will swallow you up.
25:37.note: The phrase "swallow you up" echoes the earth that swallowed rebels in Numbers 16, turning covenant curse into an image of the land itself rejecting its faithless inhabitants.

25:38.rendering: Those of you who are left will waste away in the lands of your enemies because of their guilt, and also because of the guilt of their fathers they will waste away with them.
25:38.note: The Hebrew verb "decay" or "rot away" (yimmaqqu) echoes the curses of Deuteronomy 28, painting exile as a slow withering that links generations in shared consequence.

25:39.rendering: But if they confess their iniquity and the iniquity of their fathers, in their unfaithfulness by which they acted unfaithfully against me, and also that they walked in opposition to me,
25:39.note: The Hebrew word for "opposition" (qeri) echoes the same term used for Israel's stubborn resistance in the wilderness, linking exile back to that ancient pattern of defiance.

25:40.rendering: I also walked in hostility toward them and brought them into the land of their enemies, so that their uncircumcised hearts might be humbled and they might pay for their guilt.
25:40.note: The phrase "uncircumcised hearts" is a startling wordplay on Israel's covenant sign, showing that true belonging to God has always been about the heart, not just the body.

25:41.rendering: Then I will remember my covenant with Jacob, and also my covenant with Isaac, and also my covenant with Abraham I will remember, and I will remember the land.
25:41.note: The three patriarchs appear in reverse order, a subtle literary echo that signals God circling back to the beginning of the promise.

25:42.rendering: The land will be left empty by them and will enjoy its sabbaths while it lies desolate without them. They will accept the punishment for their guilt because they rejected my rules and their soul loathed my decrees.
25:42.note: This verse echoes the land's own sabbath rest in Leviticus 25, portraying the earth as an active participant that gets its due when Israel breaks covenant.

25:43.rendering: Yet even then, when they are in the land of their enemies, I will not reject them or despise them so as to destroy them completely, breaking my covenant with them, for I am the LORD their God.
25:43.note: The Hebrew word for "reject" (ma'as) echoes God's earlier warnings of rejection in this chapter, yet here it is dramatically negated, preserving the covenant even in exile.

25:44.rendering: But I will remember on their behalf the covenant with the first ones, whom I brought out from the land of Egypt in the sight of the nations, so that I might be their God. I am the LORD.
25:44.note: This verse echoes the exodus formula almost verbatim from Exodus 6:7, showing how even covenant failure cannot erase God's ancient promise to be their God.

25:45.rendering: These are the decrees and the laws and the instructions that the LORD established between himself and the Israelites through Moses on Mount Sinai.
25:45.note: The Hebrew word for "instructions" here is torah, the same term for the entire five books, framing Leviticus as covenant gift rather than mere rulebook.
